title: CleanMCP - MCP and Context Management made Easier
summary:
📋 Overview
### 1. IDEA SUMMARY CleanMCP is a project designed to simplify the management of Model-Context Protocol (MCP) and contextual data in applications.

🎯 What it does
Clean is an MCP server that intercepts tool calls from your other MCP server calls from coding agents mid-request.

💡 Inspiration
When we started using AI coding agents like Claude Code and Cursor, we fell into a rabbit hole.

🔧 How it was built
Clean runs as an MCP server that intercepts other MCP tool calls mid-request. Every response is captured and tagged with team ID, user ID, MCP name, tool name, and timestamp.

⚡ Challenges
Claude Code does not let you simply route MCPs through a proxy.

🏆 Accomplishments
Got mid-request interception working so Clean actually captures tool calls as they happen Built our own MCP catalog from scratch in Neo4j instead of relying on external directories Shipped a polished...

📚 What we learned
MCP tooling is young and documentation is thin. We had to reverse-engineer patterns from examples and just try things until they worked.

🚀 What's next
Token optimization is first. We want to use cosine similarity to fetch only relevant context per prompt instead of everything, and summarize stored context to reduce token costs.
techStack: claude-code, cypher-query-language, date-fns, eslint, framer-motion, gemini-api, gsap, lucide, mcp-(model-context-protocol), mem-machine, neo4j, next.js-16, node.js-20+, prettier, radix-ui, react-19, react-hook-form, reagraph, shadcn/ui, tailwind-css-v4, tanstack/react-table, typescript, vercel, websocket, zod

title: Upside down survival
summary:
📋 Overview
### 1. IDEA SUMMARY "Upside Down Survival" is an innovative project designed to simulate survival scenarios in an inverted world, where traditional survival strategies are challenged.

🎯 What it does
Upsidometer monitors temperature, air quality, and sound to detect abnormal environmental conditions linked to Vecna’s presence.

💡 Inspiration
In Stranger Things, danger in the Upside Down is sensed through environmental changes rather than direct sight.

🔧 How it was built
We used multiple sensors to collect real-time data, processed each input to detect anomalies, and combined them into a single danger index.

⚡ Challenges
The main challenge was balancing sensitivity and accuracy while avoiding false alerts from normal environmental changes.It was difficult to integrate the Gemini Api Chatbot.

🏆 Accomplishments
The successful integration of Gemini Api Chatbot, inclusion of music when the person is in critical danger, real time sensor reading such as audio, temperature and air quality index.

📚 What we learned
Learned how to integrate Gemini Api Chatbot. Learned how to compute real time values using sensors.

🚀 What's next
Next, we plan to improve accuracy with adaptive thresholds, add more sensors, and expand the system into a portable or wearable solution for continuous protection.
prize: Winner Best use of Gemini AI

title: CppShell
summary:
📋 Overview
### CppShell Project Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: CppShell is a command-line interface (CLI) application built in C++ that likely provides a shell-like environment for users to execute commands, manage files, or interact with system processes.

🎯 What it does
CppShell is a fully functional command-line interpreter that replicates core shell behaviors: REPL Cycle: Processes user input in real-time. Infinite Pipelines: Chains commands (e.

💡 Inspiration
I wanted to stop trusting the "magic" of the terminal and understand the machinery.

🔧 How it was built
Built in C++ on Linux using three main architectural pillars: The Parser: A custom state-machine tokenizer that handles complex quoting and escape sequences character-by-character.

⚡ Challenges
Zombie Processes: Commands would finish but remain in the process table. I had to implement careful waitpid loops to reap them.

🏆 Accomplishments
Zero High-Level Dependencies: Built entirely with raw POSIX syscalls (unistd.h, sys/wait.

📚 What we learned
Files are Integers: I gained a deep appreciation for how Linux treats File Descriptors. Cost of Forking: Learned how expensive yet powerful cloning a process state is.

🚀 What's next
Signal Handling: Implementing Ctrl+C handling. Environment Variables: Adding export and variable expansion ($VAR).
prize: Winner 1st Place — Build From Scratch Champion
techStack: c++, git, linux, readline

title: Lumos
summary:
📋 Overview
### 1. IDEA SUMMARY Lumos is an innovative project that utilizes advanced technologies to enhance online safety and user experience.

🎯 What it does
Lumos is an AI Co-pilot for Digital Trust.

💡 Inspiration
We live in a state of constant digital alert. Every unexpected text or email forces a difficult choice: Is this a critical notification or a sophisticated trap?

🔧 How it was built
We engineered a Multi-Layered Intelligence Engine that deconstructs threats into parts for analysis. The Stack: We built a scalable backend using Node.

⚡ Challenges
OCR Accuracy on Varied Backgrounds: Extracting text reliably from screenshots with different lighting, resolutions, and compression artifacts using Tesseract.

🏆 Accomplishments
The "Glass Box" Approach: We didn't just build a black-box AI that says "Bad." We are proud of our UI that breaks down the Analysis Evidence.

📚 What we learned
Scams are Structural: We learned that while the content of scams changes, the structure (urgency + obscure link + unknown sender) remains remarkably consistent.

🚀 What's next
Lumos is just our first step toward a safer digital ecosystem.

title: EcoGuard AI
summary:

### EcoGuard AI Project Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: EcoGuard AI is an innovative project designed to leverage artificial intelligence to monitor and protect the environment.

🎯 What it does
EcoGuard AI turns everyday people into environmental protectors.

💡 Inspiration
Urban India faces serious environmental problems like air pollution, water contamination, and illegal waste dumping.

🔧 How it was built
EcoGuard AI was built using Base44 to rapidly design and deploy a working prototype within hackathon time limits.

⚡ Challenges
Limited real-world data made it hard to fully validate AI predictions inside the Base44 environment. Configuring accurate multi-language voice support through Base44 integrations was challenging.

🏆 Accomplishments
Built a fully working end-to-end prototype using Base44 in a hackathon timeline. Enabled multi-input and multi-language reporting for real-world accessibility.

📚 What we learned

summary:

### ResQ.ai Project Summary 1.

🎯 What it does
ResQ.ai continuously monitors a person using a real-time camera feed and pose estimation.

💡 Inspiration
Many seniors and people living alone experience falls that go unnoticed for long periods. We came across stories of individuals lying on the floor for 30–60 minutes before help arrived.

🔧 How it was built
Computer Vision: YOLO-Pose is used for real-time posture detection. We analyze centroid drop, velocity, and angle shifts to detect falls.

⚡ Challenges
Integrating Python backend with Flutter frontend Ensuring YOLO runs smoothly in real time. Rewriting backend schemas when switching from SQL to MongoDB.

🏆 Accomplishments
A working fall-detection pipeline with real-time inference. Clean integration from vision model to backend to mobile app.

📚 What we learned
Vision-based fall detection using pose keypoints and motion metrics. Full-stack integration using Flutter, FastAPI, and MongoDB.

🚀 What's next
Deploy on edge devices (Jetson Nano / Raspberry Pi + Coral). Train a custom dataset for improved fall accuracy.

summary:

### 1. IDEA SUMMARY The REAL Hackathon Browser Agent is a software tool designed to enhance web browsing experiences by leveraging advanced AI models.

Our agent can autonomously perform everyday browser operations such as: Applying to jobs or filling out forms Sending emails Setting calendar events Booking hotels Navigating links and multi-page flow...

This hack challenge pushed us to build an agent that can operate reliably under real-world constraints — fast, accurate, and versatile enough to handle practical browser tasks.

API credit limitations restricted our ability to extensively test iterations. Flow connection issues, especially when integrating multiple models.

Our agent successfully completed a large variety of REAL-style tasks. Despite limited time and compute, we were able to create a pipeline that runs reliably across multiple task types.

📚 What we learned
Multi-agent systems with VLMs are extremely hard to synchronize — keeping track of state, screenshots, and browser feedback loops is non-trivial.

🚀 What's next
We plan to: Polish the agent into a long-term entrant for the 3-month REAL global leaderboard challenge. Improve error recovery, latency, and batching.

title: Xploit
summary:
📋 Overview
### Xploit Project Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: Xploit is a web application designed to streamline the process of discovering and exploiting vulnerabilities in software systems.

🎯 What it does
Xploit is an automated red-teaming suite that acts as a digital sparring partner for your AI workforce.

💡 Inspiration
It is late 2025, and while companies are deploying autonomous AI agents to handle real operations and money, nobody helped developers secure the actual applications.

🔧 How it was built
We architected Xploit as a FastAPI backend orchestrating multiple specialized AI agents powered by Pydantic AI, paired with a real-time React frontend that visualizes the attack as it unfolds.

⚡ Challenges
The biggest challenge was making the multi-agent attacker system actually converge on wins instead of spinning in circles.

🏆 Accomplishments
We built the entire system from scratch in just 18 hours.

📚 What we learned
We learned that even if the underlying model is safe, giving it access to tools like APIs and databases introduces massive vulnerabilities where context becomes the new attack vector.

🚀 What's next
We aim to make Xploit the standard security certification for the Agentic web by integrating directly into CI/CD pipelines to run a security gauntlet every time a developer pushes a prompt change.
techStack: fastapi, openrouter, pydantic, python, typescript
team: Dalu Okonkwo, Tony Okeke, Michael Moemeke

title: SecureVista
summary:

### SecureVista Project Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: SecureVista is a security-oriented application that leverages advanced computer vision and face recognition technologies to enhance surveillance and user safety.

SecureVista is an autonomous surveillance pipeline that transforms standard IP cameras into intelligent guardians.

We identified a critical flaw in modern security infrastructure: the Human Latency Gap.

🔧 How it was built
We engineered a Distributed Edge-Inference Pipeline designed for high throughput.

⚡ Challenges
The "Flicker" False Positive: Initial models would flag a threat for 1 frame due to lighting noise.

🏆 Accomplishments
🏆 Winner of CodeVeda: Secured top spot in the 48hr Hackathon organized by IIT Madras BS and Manipal University Jaipur.

📚 What we learned
Data Drift: Models trained on well-lit datasets fail in low-light corridors. We learned the importance of Gamma Correction preprocessing to normalize input feeds.

🚀 What's next
Action Recognition: Moving beyond bounding boxes to Video Vision Transformers (ViViT) to detect complex interactions like fights.

TrueBite is a Chrome extension that uses Yelp AI API to introduce trusted ratings, AI-generated review insights, and better restaurant alternatives directly within DoorDash, Uber Eats, and Grubhub.

💡 Inspiration
Food delivery apps often display inflated ratings that don’t always reflect real customer experiences, forcing users to jump between platforms to verify restaurant quality.

🔧 How it was built
We built TrueBite as a Manifest V3 Chrome extension with content scripts that detect restaurant pages and extract key details like restaurant name and location.

⚡ Challenges
Each delivery platform has a different and frequently changing DOM structure, making restaurant and location detection unreliable at times.

🏆 Accomplishments
We successfully integrated Yelp AI API as the exclusive data source, built a responsive UI that works across three major delivery platforms, and created an interactive chat experience that turns stati...

📚 What we learned
We learned to use Yelp AI API to extract data. We also gained hands-on experience handling real-world challenges like fuzzy matching, rate limits, and UX constraints inside third-party websites.

🚀 What's next
Next, we plan to expand the chat experience with follow-up suggestions based on user preferences, add dietary and lifestyle filters, and introduce personalization over time.

title: AUTO-OPS: AI DevOps Agent
summary:
📋 Overview
### 1. IDEA SUMMARY AUTO-OPS: AI DevOps Agent is an innovative solution designed to automate DevOps processes using advanced AI technologies.

🎯 What it does
AUTO-OPS automatically detects runtime errors from any Python project, analyzes the code, generates a patch, tests it safely in a Daytona sandbox, and opens a pull request — all without human interven...

💡 Inspiration
We wanted to build something that eliminates the “debug fatigue” developers face — when your code breaks and you spend hours tracing logs, fixing one bug just to introduce another.

🔧 How it was built
Frontend: Next.js + Tailwind dashboard showing incoming errors and a live “agent console” timeline.

⚡ Challenges
Coordinating six separate APIs under one asynchronous workflow. Keeping Claude’s reasoning “safe” so it never writes destructive patches.

Built a fully autonomous debugging pipeline that touches every partner API. AUTO-OPS can realistically detect, fix, test, and commit a live Python error.

📚 What we learned
How to combine multiple AI systems — Claude, CodeRabbit, ElevenLabs — into a cohesive workflow that doesn’t collapse under API latency.

🚀 What's next
Introduce real-time VS Code integration so the agent appears as an in-editor assistant. Build team dashboards for organizations to monitor agent performance across repos.

title: SafeWalk+
summary:
📋 Overview
### SafeWalk+ Project Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: SafeWalk+ is a mobile and web application designed to enhance personal safety during walks, particularly in urban environments.

🎯 What it does
SafeWalk+ calculates routes for walking or cycling by balancing safety and efficiency.

💡 Inspiration
SafeWalk+ was inspired by the need for safer urban navigation for pedestrians and cyclists.

🔧 How it was built
We built SafeWalk+ using a React frontend with Leaflet for interactive maps and a Node.js/Express backend for route calculations.

⚡ Challenges
One major challenge was ensuring the safest and fastest routes were distinct—initially they were identical because we hadn’t separated distance and safety scoring in the algorithm.

🏆 Accomplishments
We are proud of building a fully functional MVP that can calculate and display safe and fast routes on a map, even with limited data.

📚 What we learned
We learned how to integrate geospatial data from CSVs into a graph structure for routing, and how to apply weighted scoring to balance multiple factors like safety and distance.

🚀 What's next
Next, we plan to refine the backend algorithm to include real-time traffic data, dynamic crime alerts, and better time-of-day adjustments.

### RENEW'D Project Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: RENEW'D is a web application designed to promote sustainability by helping users track and reduce their carbon footprint.

🎯 What it does
Renew'd reimagines thrifting as an effortless, personalized experience. Users swipe through items matched to their style, sizing, and location—no cash, no shipping costs, just direct trades.

Fast fashion is killing the planet. Millions of tons of textiles hit landfills every year while perfectly good clothes sit unworn in closets.

We started by mapping complete user flows from sign-up through listing creation, swiping, matching, and trade completion.

Balancing algorithm feature weights across heterogeneous data types was complex—location needs distance-based scoring, style requires categorical matching, sizing demands precision.

We successfully translated dating app engagement mechanics into a functional trading marketplace that solves real environmental problems.

📚 What we learned
We gained hands-on experience with recommendation systems, feature engineering, and multi-dimensional scoring algorithms.

🚀 What's next
We're completing the MVP and launching a beta to gather real user data for algorithm refinement.

title: StationScope
summary:
📋 Overview
### StationScope Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: StationScope is a project designed to enhance the accessibility and understanding of public transportation data.

🎯 What it does
It is a fully functional dynamic app. You type in the desired address in the first landing screen, and Google Maps API helps you find a valid address.

💡 Inspiration
Finding apartments as an intern or new grad moving to NYC is a daunting task. I know it.

🔧 How it was built
To develop the app, we had two main workstreams: the frontend + data modeling in R which Max handled, and the agentic bot and map, which Daren handled.

⚡ Challenges
Coordinating our differing expertises: Daren has more skills in fullstack development, Max has more skills in data science. Smoothly integrating the APIs took some time and a lot of iteration.

🏆 Accomplishments
This is Max's first hackathon!

📚 What we learned
Always plan for more time than it seems. Bugs pop up out of nowhere all the time.

🚀 What's next
Making the AI model even more robust. It turned out pretty well, we think, having only one day (and long night) to work on it, though.
prize: Winner Education; Winner Aristotle Challenge: Most Cutting-Edge AI Agent
techStack: css, geocoding, google-maps, javascript, json, mapquest, python, r, typescript, us-census-bureau

title: Study Desk Assistant
summary:
📋 Overview
### 1. IDEA SUMMARY The Study Desk Assistant is an innovative project designed to enhance the study environment by monitoring and optimizing conditions for better focus and productivity.

🎯 What it does
The Study Desk assistant utilizes sensor readings (temperature, humidity, light, sound) and data and displays them to a dashboard easily accessible by the user to get a quick look at their environment...

💡 Inspiration
Many students struggle with being focused and productive and unbeknownst to them, it can be tied back to their study environment.

🔧 How it was built
I began by utilizing sensors, DHT11 for the temperature and humidity, Photoresistor for the light level, and a KY-037 for the sound level.

⚡ Challenges
The one of the biggest challenge I had run into was the creation of the phone notification and email tips system.

🏆 Accomplishments
Though I had built multiple projects dealing with sensors and graphs, this was the first time I had properly incorporated all of them together into one seamless app.

🚀 What's next
I would love to add Alexa integration to this project, eliminating the need of the distraction of looking at another app in the middle of your work to get the sensor values of your room.
techStack: arduino, ardunio-uno, c++, chatgpt, dht11, javascript, ky-037, node-red, photoresistor, raspberry-pi

title: DocSimplified

DocSimplified is a multilingual AI-powered web application that simplifies government documents.

Delivering a fully functional, end-to-end document simplification and form-filling experience. Building a genuinely accessible tool with translation, easy summaries, and text-to-speech baked in.

title: BuckBounty
summary:

### 1. IDEA SUMMARY BuckBounty is a fintech project designed to leverage advanced AI technologies to enhance financial insights and decision-making for users.

🎯 What it does
BuckBounty is an AI-powered personal finance assistant that: Maximizes savings through credit card reward optimization (analyzes your spending patterns and recommends the best cards for each category)...

We noticed a common problem: people leave thousands of dollars on the table every year through suboptimal credit card usage, missed deals, and lack of financial awareness.

🔧 How it was built
Frontend: Next.

⚡ Challenges
Vector search scalability: Initially, semantic search on 10,000+ transactions was slow.

🏆 Accomplishments
50x performance improvement through intelligent Redis caching Multi-agent AI orchestration that actually works in production Dual-index RAG system handling 10,000+ transactions with sub-second search...

📚 What we learned
RAG architecture: Implementing FAISS with dual indexing taught us the tradeoffs between exact and approximate search.

🚀 What's next
Machine learning for budget predictions: Train models on transaction history to predict future spending and warn users before they overspend.

summary:

### 1. IDEA SUMMARY Saathi is a web application designed to streamline document management and collaboration, leveraging interactive data visualizations and efficient file processing.

🎯 What it does
At first glance, Saathi may seem just another chatbot that can generate quizzes. But what if it actually reduces cognitive dependency on AI and can help humans grow?

💡 Inspiration
With the rise of vibe coding, almost everyone—including me, at some point—are or have been stuck in this AI hell where the lines of knowing something and actually being able to do it blur.

🔧 How it was built
Frontend: Built with Tailwind CSS. The static HTML/CSS layouts were generated using Stitch to speed up UI development, allowing me to focus on the logic.

⚡ Challenges
*Supabase's RLS policies: * It prevent unauthenticated users from inserting data into tables like profiles. But new users need a profile row created before they can authenticate.

🏆 Accomplishments
Building all of this solo: Of course the project making could be way smoother if I had a teammate, but looking back and realizing I did all of this (code, documentation, demo, filling out the Devpost...

📚 What we learned
*Find a team, with the same timezone: * Time zone, no matter what you do, always is a bit of a barrier sometimes when it comes to meetings and ideation, regardless a team helps a lot.

🚀 What's next
Spaced repetition, flashcard generation, automatic resource finder, and integration with FocusAI, another EdTech project I have that can go well with Saathi.
techStack: chart.js, django, gemini-api, javascript, postgresql, pymupdf, python, python-docx, supabase, tailwind

summary:

### Pancake Pals Project Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: Pancake Pals is a mobile application designed to enhance social interactions through cooking, specifically focusing on pancake-making.

Pancake Pals tracks your screen time and turns it into a daily challenge. The less time you spend on your phone, the more breakfast points you earn.

💡 Inspiration
We wanted to make screen time fun instead of guilty. Everyone has tried to “use their phone less” and failed — so we thought, why not turn it into a game?

🔧 How it was built
We built the app using Flutter + Dart so it could run cross-platform. We used Android Usage Stats to read screen time, and Firebase for storing user progress and friend leaderboard data.

⚡ Challenges
It was our first time working with flutter and dart, so it took us longer than expected to get set up with it.

🏆 Accomplishments
We got real screen-time tracking working and fully connected it with the reward system and characters.

📚 What we learned
We learned a ton about Android permission APIs, Flutter build configurations, and debugging emulator chaos.

🚀 What's next
We want to add more breakfast characters, weekly tournaments, and streak bonuses.

title: AccessiGesture
summary:
📋 Overview
### AccessiGesture Project Summary 1. IDEA SUMMARY: AccessiGesture is a project aimed at enhancing accessibility through gesture recognition technology.

🎯 What it does
AccessiGesture runs as a lightweight, unobtrusive application that translates a user's hand movements into direct, real-time cursor control.

💡 Inspiration
In a world that runs on digital interaction, the mouse and keyboard remain a fundamental barrier for millions.

🔧 How it was built
The project was built entirely in Python, which served as the "glue" for a stack of powerful, specialized libraries, each chosen for a specific purpose to create a seamless, real-time experience.

⚡ Challenges
Our development process was a story of hitting walls and finding breakthroughs. Our first challenge was Framework Selection.

🏆 Accomplishments
This project was a journey of firsts, but a few accomplishments stand out: The Visual Debugger: We are incredibly proud of the real-time diagnostic overlay.

📚 What we learned
This project was a masterclass in the architecture of real-time computer vision applications. Stateful Program Design: We learned the critical importance of managing "state.

🚀 What's next
Trainable Models: Our next big leap is to move from our hard-coded (heuristic) gesture rules to a proper machine-learned model.

title: SeniCare
summary:
🎯 What it does
SeniCare is a role-based web app designed to help clinics monitor seniors who live alone or remotely through a simple daily check-in. Seniors complete a guided daily check-in.

💡 Inspiration
We wanted to build something genuinely useful for clinics that care for seniors, especially older patients who live alone, are remote, or do not have someone consistently watching over their day-to-da...

⚡ Challenges
Synchronizing real-time phases across voice and video Ensuring camera scan timing aligns with the AI prompt timing.

🏆 Accomplishments
Built a true end-to-end multimodal pipeline, not just mock screens. Implemented role-specific product surfaces (senior experience and clinician analytics/reporting).

📚 What we learned
Reliable health-adjacent UX depends heavily on state orchestration and clear transition rules. A simple UI still requires complex backend sequencing to be trustworthy.

🚀 What's next
Add longitudinal baseline and trend modeling per senior (not just per-check-in triage). Replace browser-dependent speech handling with a more robust STT pipeline.

title: Health Web Services
summary:
🎯 What it does
HWS (Health Web Services) is a full-stack app that lets you: Connect a Solana wallet (Solflare) to authenticate actions.

💡 Inspiration
Healthcare records are some of the most sensitive data people have, yet patients and providers still deal with fragmented systems, slow sharing, and limited transparency about who accessed what.

🔧 How it was built
Frontend (React + Vite): Wallet connection via @solana/wallet-adapter-* (Solflare). Canonicalizes the record payload and computes a SHA-256 hash.

⚡ Challenges
Wallet UX + state: handling wallet connection edge cases and making “store/retrieve requires wallet” behavior consistent.

🏆 Accomplishments
End-to-end workflow: wallet-authenticated store → encrypted off-chain storage → authorized retrieval → on-chain audit proofs.

📚 What we learned
How to use Solana wallets not just for “login”, but as a source of verifiable, timestamped audit proofs. Practical tradeoffs between blockchain transparency and healthcare privacy.

🚀 What's next
Stronger verification: tighten the proof format and expand on-chain verification beyond simple memos.
techStack: c, css, digitalocean, javascript, powershell, solana
team: Derek Ji, Harry Xue, Benson Wang

title: Recall
summary:

Identify - Live camera feed with real-time face recognition. Point the device at someone and see their name and relationship.

💡 Inspiration

🔧 How it was built
Frontend: Vite, React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS. Face recognition in the browser with face-api.

Echo / feedback loop: The mic was picking up the app’s own voice (ElevenLabs), so the “user” transcript was our reply and the loop repeated. We fixed it by adding a 2.

🏆 Accomplishments
Real conversation in Calm Mode - Not a pre-written script: the app listens, sends what the user said to Groq, and speaks a new reply every time.

title: Pro Pro Sahur
summary:
🎯 What it does
It detects when a user opens a tab or application it deems as distracting and tells you to stop before going up to the tab and closing it itself.

💡 Inspiration
The whole team consists of chronic procrastinators, so we thought it would be a nice idea to look after ourselves in a light-hearted fashion.

🔧 How it was built
We built it using the .NET 8 WPF (Windows Presentation Foundation) and it checks the foreground window title against blocklist via Win32 GetForegroundWindow / GetWindowText.

⚡ Challenges
Ran into problems implementing the AI text to speech. Also animating the character walking up to the tab was choppy.

🏆 Accomplishments
The team is proud of making a simple and fun project that could genuinely be useful to people that have problems with procrastination, and doing so in such a small period of time.

📚 What we learned
Learned how to integrate AI into any project, and learned of the vast amount of AI tools that exist across the internet.

Over 2 billion people worldwide live with vision impairments, yet core assistive tools like white canes have seen little innovation.

I built WayFinder with a local-first edge AI architecture. A quantized multi-billion-parameter VLM runs directly on a mobile device for offline inference.

⚡ Challenges
The biggest challenge was system integration on mobile edge devices. Running large VLMs required overcoming memory limits, framework incompatibilities, and latency constraints.

🏆 Accomplishments
Running a multi-billion-parameter VLM locally on a mobile device, enabling real-time, offline navigation assistance. Designing a privacy-preserving civic data pipeline.

📚 What we learned
I learned that impactful AI is not just about model size, but where intelligence lives. Edge AI enables privacy, reliability, and accessibility that cloud-only systems can’t.

🚀 What's next
Next, I plan to further reduce latency and improve real-time feedback through better model optimization.

More scenarios. Currently three (breakup, meeting, job loss); want to ship a fourth before anyone reasonable can stop us.

🎯 What it does
Aegis Edge is an air-gapped autonomous defense for embedded hardware.

💡 Inspiration
Hospitals, forward operating bases, and SCADA plants can't ship sensor data to the cloud, HIPAA, denied-comms, air-gaps. So when an attacker spoofs a sensor on the wire, no SOC ever sees it.

🔧 How it was built

🏆 Accomplishments
End-to-end recovery in under a few seconds: attack → detect → generate → flash → vent breathing again. A fully untethered attacker.

📚 What we learned
Physical-layer attacks change the threat model entirely; software defenses have to assume the wires are lying.

🚀 What's next
Multi-sensor fusion: derive any dropped channel from the survivors, not just temperature. Signed firmware so the reflash itself can't be hijacked.

ASI-1 re-ranking vs. latency budgets.

title: SPECTRA
summary:
🎯 What it does
SPECTRA turns the iPhone Pro's sparse 192×256 LiDAR signal into a dense 768×1024 metric depth map, in real time, on-device. It has two halves: SPECTRANet — an RGB-guided depth upsampler.

💡 Inspiration
Depth perception is the bottleneck for almost every embodied AI system — robots, AR headsets, accessibility tools, autonomous vehicles.

🔧 How it was built
The model. A RGBGuidedDepthUpsampler (SPECTRANet/spectranet/model.

⚡ Challenges
Coordinate systems. ARKit's depth map is rotated relative to the RGB frame depending on device orientation; the confidence mask is yet another grid.

🏆 Accomplishments
A 2 MB depth model that runs on a phone and visibly beats Apple's bicubic baseline on edges — without sacrificing metric accuracy.

📚 What we learned
Edge GPUs like the GX10 are real. Training a non-trivial vision model outside a datacenter is now genuinely practical, and the same checkpoint deploys to phones unchanged.

🚀 What's next
Temporal consistency. Right now each frame is upsampled independently, which causes subtle flicker.
prize: Winner Track: Flicker To Flow; Winner Zetic Company Challenge

We started this project from a simple but high‑impact question: why are floor plans everywhere, yet blind and low‑vision users often get zero usable orientation from them?

Python + FastAPI backend to handle uploads, map creation, and serving /m/{map_id} chat + /m/{map_id}/voice.

⚡ Challenges
Latency from chaining model calls; we redesigned the pipeline so “broadcast Q&A” can skip regeneration when a tactile image already exists.

🏆 Accomplishments
A true end-to-end accessibility pipeline: floorplan → tactile output → QR → Q&A (text + voice).

📚 What we learned
Great accessibility UX comes from grounded context and simple interaction loops (scan → ask → orient).

🚀 What's next
Improve map understanding with structured layouts (doors/walls/POIs) so Q&A can answer with higher precision and fewer vision-only guesses.

title: Aesthesis
summary:

Upload a demo and Aesthesis tells you, second by second, how a human brain would respond to it.

Every UX team in the world is testing the wrong thing. Heatmaps tell you where users hovered.

Three services connected by HTTP: a Next.js 16 frontend (Framer Motion, Recharts, react-three-fiber), a FastAPI orchestrator, and a TRIBE GPU service running on Modal A100s.

⚡ Challenges
Making a research model fast enough to demo. Our first end-to-end run took 148 seconds to analyze a 14-second clip — a state-of-the-art GPU running 11× slower than the source video.

🏆 Accomplishments
Took state of the art research and turned it into a real, usable product Built a real anatomical brain, not a stock 3D blob.

📚 What we learned
Honest UX matters more than impressive numbers. When you're presenting brain data, accuracy of presentation matters as much as accuracy of the model.

🚀 What's next
Capture & Assess (autonomous demos). Create an agent that takes a URL, navigates the experience for you, and records the session, so users don't have to record their own MP4.

title: FallBack
summary:
🎯 What it does
FallBack is a passive fall detection system that removes the camera-or-wearable tradeoff entirely. A wall-mounted mmWave radar sensor paired with an ESP32 monitors movement continuously.

💡 Inspiration
Falls are the leading cause of injury death among adults over 65. In Singapore, they account for more than 85% of elderly trauma cases.

🔧 How it was built
Sensing layer: An LD2410 mmWave radar board + ESP32 reads presence, motion intensity, and fall posture. The ESP32 broadcasts numerical telemetry over BLE — no images, no audio, no footage.

🏆 Accomplishments
A live hardware-to-dashboard loop.

📚 What we learned
The dignity problem is the product problem. The technical challenge of fall detection is largely solved.

🚀 What's next
Clinical validation. Partner with a care facility to run a structured deployment and benchmark false positive and negative rates against current wearable standards.

Getting consistent turbidity scores from a consumer webcam across different lighting conditions — solved by adding a calibration baseline and blending multiple features (sharpness, saturation, blue/re...

A full end-to-end pipeline (sensor → serial → backend → WebSocket → UI → AI) that runs live at 2 Hz without glitches.

📚 What we learned
How much of "industrial" monitoring is really just good UX on top of cheap sensors.

🚀 What's next
Closed-loop control — let the AI assistant propose parameter changes and, with user approval, actually push them to the Arduino.

title: Provenance — Social Shopping for Phia
summary:
🎯 What it does
Provenance takes any product URL from any store, any platform, secondhand or retail, and returns a complete sentiment profile sourced from public reviews across the internet.

💡 Inspiration
I shop secondhand constantly. And every time I find something I love on Depop or Poshmark, I hit the same wall.

🔧 How it was built
The pipeline has three steps. First, the URL is sent to Claude, which infers the product name, brand, and category from the URL structure alone with no page scraping required.

⚡ Challenges
The biggest early blocker was the scraping wall. The original approach used BeautifulSoup to fetch product page HTML, but Depop, Poshmark, and Zara all block bot requests and return empty pages.

🏆 Accomplishments
Building a fully functional, real-time AI pipeline from scratch in under 24 hours, solo!

📚 What we learned
Less is more. Dropping the scraping layer entirely made Provenance faster, more reliable, and more elegant.

🚀 What's next
A few things I'd build with the Phia team with more time. First, a Chrome extension so Provenance works directly on any product page without needing to copy and paste a URL.

title: Tideline
summary:
🎯 What it does
Tideline predicts marine heatwave risk for the San Diego coastline at 3-day and 7-day horizons.

💡 Inspiration
At Team HydroRF, we recently accomplished a ranking of #1 USA & Top-5 Global for The EY AI & Data Challenge, where we built a machine learning model to predict water quality in South Africa!

🔧 How it was built
We narrowed the project to the San Diego coast and fused multiple datasets into a single forecasting pipeline.

⚡ Challenges
Our data came from different sources, formats, and cadences- buoys were hourly point measurements, satellites were daily rasters, and Scripps/CalCOFI data were lower-frequency profiles.

🏆 Accomplishments
We achieved strong predictive performance on both horizons, with 0.8946 AUC at 3 days and 0.

📚 What we learned
We learned that climate tools become much more compelling when they are tied to a narrow user, a specific decision, and a short time horizon. It is easier to work up from a niche to a generalization.

🚀 What's next
Next, we want to add more kelp-specific and Scripps-derived features, test the model on more historical marine heatwave events, and pilot the product with a real San Diego kelp operator.

Tribe v2 inference latency — visual scoring was too slow at request time, so we pre-inferred the Nuuly catalog offline. Keeping four stylist agents distinct without fighting over the same SKUs.

Multi-agent systems live or die on state discipline. The real ML unlock is knowing what to precompute.

Deeper Tribe v2 training on every new claim. Platform hooks: every Airbnb booking or wedding invite becomes a trip.

Every large pharma company sits on a graveyard of shelved drug programs molecules that cleared Phase I safety, showed real biological activity, then got killed for reasons that had almost nothing to d...

Lazarus is architected as a live control plane, not a notebook. Backend.

Streaming reasoning over WebSocket while persisting to Postgres without blocking the orchestrator turned into a surprisingly subtle async problem.

🏆 Accomplishments
A reasoning trace you can actually watch. Every agent step is persisted and pushed over WebSocket the UI isn't a progress spinner pretending to be intelligence; it's a live, inspectable DAG.

📚 What we learned
Explicit orchestration beats ReAct for auditable science. A typed agent DAG cost us some flexibility but bought reliable, citable traces which is the product in a regulated domain.

🚀 What's next
Redis-backed Celery / RQ workers for long-running async runs, replacing the current threaded executor so the pipeline can scale to portfolio-wide scans.

title: ColoMap
summary:
🎯 What it does
It takes a colorectal cancer biopsy slide, breaks it into thousands of small patches, and classifies each one into one of 9 tissue types.

💡 Inspiration
Pathologists today have to manually scan enormous microscope slides to find cancerous tissue — it's slow, subjective, and error prone.

🔧 How it was built
We fine-tuned a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network pretrained on ImageNet, training it on the NCT-CRC-HE-100K dataset of 4,500 H&E-stained histology patches.

⚡ Challenges
We had no GPU — everything trained on CPU, which meant long iteration cycles and careful decisions about dataset size.

🏆 Accomplishments
Reaching 95% accuracy on CRC-VAL-HE-7K — a benchmark drawn from different patients and different scanners than the training data — without any stain normalization.

📚 What we learned
Transfer learning works surprisingly well on medical imaging even with modest data.

🚀 What's next
Push accuracy from 95% toward the 97% target with longer training and tuning. Add stain normalization so the model generalizes better across hospitals and scanners.

Every night, medical writers at pharma companies paste confidential clinical trial documents into ChatGPT to clean up the grammar.

🔧 How it was built
Architecture. A modular Python pipeline with strict separation of concerns.

⚡ Challenges
The embedding–text privacy gap. This is the headline finding, and it surfaced by accident.

🏆 Accomplishments
A mechanistic negative result that generalizes. Formal $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP on hidden-state representations does not propagate to text-surface privacy under an unmodified decoder.

🚀 What's next
Decoder-aware DP. Perturbation along the decoder's output-sensitive directions rather than isotropic noise, informed by mechanistic interpretability of the decoder's representation structure.

Quant research is powerful but painful — messy Jupyter notebooks, hidden biases like lookahead leakage and survivorship bias in yfinance, and backtests that obscure the real theory.

Small math decisions have outsized consequences — log returns vs. pct_change, proper lagging, and frequency-aware Sharpe all meaningfully shift results.

Full multi-asset support, a hosted version with pipeline sharing, and expanded blocks for live data and advanced models.

title: The Real RoboTO
summary:
🎯 What it does
It powers a Martian habitat for 7 sols straight.

💡 Inspiration
Keeping people alive on Mars means you need power and there's no grid out there, no backup generator you can call.

🔧 How it was built
Everything was done in MATLAB/Simulink. First we swept through different solar panel sizes and battery SOC levels to find the sweet spot (50 kW, 90% initial SOC).

⚡ Challenges
The controller tuning was painful. Tiny changes in the gains would make the whole thing respond not correctly.

🏆 Accomplishments
The full system actually worked 7 sols, zero interruptions. Voltage stayed in the safe zone the entire time and the battery behaved.

📚 What we learned
Honestly we learned how much tuning matters. The theory is one thing but getting the gains right in simulation is a whole different game.

🚀 What's next
We want to develop an algorithm that auto-tunes the controller gains instead of doing it manually.

Tavily's date filtering counts backward from today rather than supporting true date ranges, which made per-quarter isolation unreliable for historical queries.

summary:

Bookmarky turns bookmarks into floating bubbles governed by physics. Every time you click a link, its bubble grows.

💡 Inspiration
Most bookmark managers treat links as data, flat lists sorted alphabetically or by date.

🔧 How it was built
Built as a single React component using Framer's AI. The physics used: center attraction, inter-bubble repulsion, boundary collision, and friction all calculated per frame.

⚡ Challenges
Getting drag and click to coexist on the same element without false triggers.

🏆 Accomplishments

CORS in the Cashu ecosystem. No mints set CORS headers — every browser-side probe was blocked.

The math works in practice. The $8.

Uncertainty matters more than the score itself. Modeling as distributions rather than point estimates changed every downstream decision and made the system degrade gracefully under sparse data.

title: Replay
summary:
🎯 What it does
Replay captures a real-world 3D environment and human body motion in real time, then lets you replay it as an interactive 3D scene.

💡 Inspiration
Everyone who's played Minecraft knows Replay Mod — you record a session, then scrub through time and fly a free camera around the 3D world to relive it from any angle.

🔧 How it was built
Capture hardware: An iPhone 13 Pro runs a custom Swift/ARKit app that streams LiDAR point cloud frames, VIO/SLAM world position, and IMU quaternions over WebSocket via USB tether.

⚡ Challenges
Synchronizing and orienting independent data streams was the hardest problem — especially relating the ESP32 IMU data to the iPhone's VIO coordinate frame.

🏆 Accomplishments
The fidelity-to-sensor ratio.

📚 What we learned
Quaternion math is humbling. We went from "how hard can rotation be" to implementing swing-twist decomposition and Madgwick filters from papers.

🚀 What's next
Adding more IMU nodes for full-body tracking while maintaining our sensor-to-output ratio.

🎯 What it does
Users sign up for OurCampus, upload their schedules and tell about themselves. Taking this data into account, our algorithm matches users together.

💡 Inspiration
At one point or another, we were all sitting around campus, looking for people to spend our breaks with.

🔧 How it was built
Brian build the full-stack app using Next.js 14 with TypeScript.

⚡ Challenges
Attempting to combine the different parts of the application together. Implementing the heatmap into the main app was difficult due to having to convert the HTML to React.

title: Indicium
summary:
🎯 What it does
Indicium is a real estate data platform that pulls data from multiple public sources, cleans and normalizes it, and displays it through a geospatial dashboard.

💡 Inspiration
Real estate analysis today is slow, fragmented, and heavily reliant on manual data collection from multiple disconnected sources.

🔧 How it was built
We built a data ingestion pipeline that pulls from sources like FRED, Census ACS, HUD, and local permit APIs, then processes and standardizes the data into a unified format.

⚡ Challenges
One of the biggest challenges was normalizing data from different sources that all had different formats, update frequencies, and levels of granularity.

🏆 Accomplishments
We successfully built a working end to end pipeline that pulls live data, processes it, and visualizes it in a clean and interactive dashboard.

📚 What we learned
We learned how to work with large public datasets and deal with inconsistencies across different data sources.

🚀 What's next
Next, we want to expand the number of supported markets and add more data layers like vacancy rates, pricing trends, and predictive analytics.
techStack: antigravity, api, flask, gemini, google-cloud, node.js, python, react, typescript, vite
team: Jack CS, OwenPainter Painter, Lambert Edigin

title: RateMyWater
summary:
🎯 What it does
RateMyWater lets anyone rate and review any body of water in the world — rivers, lakes, beaches, ponds, oceans — on a scale from Dive In With Mouth Open (5/5) to Biohazard Speed Run (1/5).

💡 Inspiration
Have you ever walked up to a lake, river, or beach and wondered — can I actually get in this? One of our team members didn't get the chance to ask that question.

🔧 How it was built
We started with research — understanding the real problem, identifying what information people actually needed, and mapping out the features that would solve it.

⚡ Challenges
Deciding which features to prioritize was harder than we expected — we had a long list of real problems we wanted to solve and not enough time to tackle all of them.

🏆 Accomplishments
We built something real that solves a real problem, and we did it fast. We're proud of how the rating scale turned out — instantly readable, no jargon, just honest human reviews.

📚 What we learned
Beyond the personal lessons, we picked up a stack of new technical skills: working with MongoDB including fuzzy search, autocomplete, and vector search for intelligent data retrieval; building and str...

🚀 What's next
The dream doesn't stop here.

title: ForeSight
summary:
🎯 What it does
ForeSight is a hyper-intelligent, predictive startup consultant. You sit down and go through a dynamic, 20-question adaptive interview.

💡 Inspiration
It is a brutal, well-known statistic that 90% of startups fail. Founders often spend years of their lives and thousands of dollars building products that nobody actually wants.

🔧 How it was built
The frontend was built using React and Vite, utilizing a complex architecture of CSS modules to create a glass-morphic, dark-mode visual experience. Our backend runs on Python and FastAPI.

⚡ Challenges
Parsing unstructured, conversational interview text into rigid, integer-based rubric scores was incredibly tough.

🏆 Accomplishments
We completely bridged the gap between generative language models and hard, quantitative machine learning algorithms.

📚 What we learned
We learned a massive amount about prompt engineering constraints. Forcing an LLM to be extremely harsh, demand numbers, and explicitly forbid vague jargon requires very strict systemic boundaries.

🚀 What's next
We want to integrate real-time financial tracking.

title: Paper Zoo
summary:
🎯 What it does
Paper Zoo is an end-to-end origami learning platform. Users select a model (from simple chicks to complex dragons), follow AI-generated tutorials, and use their camera to verify each fold.

💡 Inspiration
The inspiration for Paper Zoo came from our childhood memories of what activities and games we found difficult, and what we could implement to ease those difficulties.

🔧 How it was built
We built Paper Zoo during Wildhacks 2026 using a modern, scalable stack: Frontend: Next.js 16 with Tailwind CSS for a fun, interactive aesthetic for children.

⚡ Challenges
Prompt Hallucination in Vision: Early iterations of the Vision API were too generous. We had to implement a "Strict Grading" philosophy in our verify.

🏆 Accomplishments
End-to-End Magic: Seeing a user fold a real piece of paper, take a photo, and then see a pixel-art version of that exact bird "pop" into their digital zoo is a magical UX moment.

📚 What we learned
UI for Children Matters: Moving from a generic bootstrap-style UI to a child-friendly, interactive design increased our fun factor, which kept us motivated during the late-night sessions.

🚀 What's next
AR Overlays: Using WebXR to project diagram lines directly onto the user's paper through their phone screen.

title: Research-RAG
summary:
🎯 What it does
Drop your PDFs into a web app, ask a question, get a written answer back with every claim linked to the exact passage it came from.

💡 Inspiration
Every research project starts with a folder of PDFs that just keeps growing. You know the answer is in there somewhere, but Cmd+F fails and Google doesn't know which papers you have.

🔧 How it was built
Frontend: React + Vite + Tailwind — upload, ask, read. Backend: a small Node/Express server that handles uploads and talks to Gemini.

⚡ Challenges
"The biggest one: the RAG kept returning the wrong answers.

🏆 Accomplishments
It actually cites its sources — every answer links back to a real passage. The hybrid search runs in a single database query, so the whole thing stays small and fast.

📚 What we learned
Pure semantic search isn't enough on its own — you need exact-word search too. The order matters: filter before you rank, not after.

🚀 What's next
Support more than PDFs (arXiv links, notes, Word docs). Follow-up questions that remember the last one.

title: Voyager
summary:
🎯 What it does
Voyager is an interactive rebuild of NASA’s Voyager Reed-Solomon error correction pipeline.

💡 Inspiration
Deep space communication is a one-shot problem. When a spacecraft sends data back from billions of kilometres away, retransmission may be impossible.

🔧 How it was built
Voyager has two parallel recovery pipelines, one for images and one for text, both built around a shared RS(255,223) codec.

⚡ Challenges
Our main challenge was making Voyager mathematically faithful without losing clarity.

🏆 Accomplishments
We’re proud that Voyager is not just a visual demo, but a working and fully observable Reed-Solomon recovery system.

📚 What we learned
We learned that observability matters.

🚀 What's next
The next step is to strengthen Voyager technically and extend it to more types of data.

Floods are the number one natural disaster globally, yet most safety tools rely on a stable power grid and high-speed internet—the first two things that fail in an emergency.

📚 What we learned
This project was a deep dive into the Power of the PWA.

🚀 What's next
Mesh-Network Messaging: Implementing WebRTC for peer-to-peer signaling when cell towers are down.

title: CarbonPlay
summary:
🎯 What it does
CarbonPlay generates full-season schedules for NBA, NHL, and NFL, optimizing for lower travel-related emissions while balancing fairness (rest, burden spread) and density rules. The Next.

💡 Inspiration
Professional sports seasons mean huge amounts of team travel and emissions. Scheduling affects miles flown, rest, and fairness.

🔧 How it was built
Backend: Python FastAPI, core logic in optimizer.py (league-specific matchups + greedy multi-objective search), orchestration and optional baseline in service.

⚡ Challenges
Hosting limits: Long NBA/NHL optimizations exceeded HTTP timeouts on free hosting so we reduced optimizer restarts

🏆 Accomplishments
End-to-end product: real optimizer + polished UI + live deploy.

📚 What we learned
Greedy full-season search is CPU-heavy; serverless-style timeouts are a real design constraint for 82-game leagues.

🚀 What's next
Expanding to MLB and MLS Async optimization: for results so long MLB/NBA/NHL runs work reliably on cheap hosts.

summary:

Kaimon lets you point your camera at any physical object and handles the entire purchase automatically.

The pipeline has a few distinct layers that all have to talk to each other in real time.

Coordinating nine agents without them stepping on each other was harder than expected.

title: LungIQ
summary:
🎯 What it does
LungIQ turns a CT scan and patient records into an evidence-grounded starting point for lung cancer treatment planning.

💡 Inspiration
Across U.S.

🔧 How it was built
Layer Choice Frontend React + Vite + Tailwind CSS for a fast, responsive clinical dashboard with pipeline visibility and interactive case exploration.

⚡ Challenges
The hardest part was building something trustworthy under hackathon constraints, especially with a small synthetic case base of about 150 patients rather than a real hospital-scale dataset.

🏆 Accomplishments
We built a working prototype that goes beyond “ask an LLM and hope” by grounding outputs in tumor data, similar cases, and supporting evidence.

📚 What we learned
We learned that in clinical AI, retrieval, provenance, and traceability matter just as much as generation quality.

title: Veritas
summary:
🎯 What it does
Veritas is a clinical research platform that combines cryptographic proof-of-personhood with ML-powered response quality scoring to give researchers clean, trustworthy data.

💡 Inspiration
The global clinical trials market is worth over $80 billion. A single failed Phase III trial costs around $350 million.

🔧 How it was built
Stack: Next.js 14 (App Router), React 19, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, PostgreSQL via Supabase (raw SQL), NextAuth.

⚡ Challenges
The hardest design problem was making AI feel collaborative to the participant. Real-time validity nudges say things like "your answer seems vague, want to add more?

🏆 Accomplishments
Clinical research fraud is a problem that is perfectly suited for proof-of-personhood technology. One person, one enrollment, enforced cryptographically.

📚 What we learned
Building for researchers taught us that good fraud detection should be invisible to participants. The scoring pipeline runs entirely post-submission.

🚀 What's next
Longitudinal study support, multi-site trial coordination, and integration with existing research platforms like REDCap.

summary:
🎯 What it does
VIE is a free web tool that makes facial recognition systems unable to identify anyone in your photos — while the image looks completely unchanged to the human eye. Upload a photo.

🔧 How it was built
Frontend: React + Tailwind CSS for uploading and per-face privacy scores.

⚡ Challenges
The core tension: how little can you change an image so a human notices nothing but an AI is completely fooled?

🏆 Accomplishments
Existing tools like Fawkes [1] and LowKey [2] only protect a single face against one or a small ensemble of models, or attempt to poison training data.

📚 What we learned

State-Resistant Threat Model: Targeted deletion is mathematically improbable.

📚 What we learned
We learned the complexities of decentralized consensus.

Index Server: A searchable database for magnet links and priorities. Leaf/Leech Application: Dedicated Mobile and Desktop interfaces.

Treating a 3D scene as just another layer in react allowed us to quickly iterate ideas and features that we wanted to implement.

title: Sensus
summary:
🎯 What it does
Sensus is a voice-first Ubuntu assistant that can: Understand spoken commands and route them to the right capability (browser, desktop actions, shell, shortcuts, or coding).

💡 Inspiration
Sensus was inspired by a simple question: what would a truly voice-first computer experience look like for someone who cannot rely on a screen?

🔧 How it was built
We built Sensus as a modular Python system: Orchestrator: OpenAI-compatible model routing and tool-calling logic for deciding what action to take.

⚡ Challenges
Making voice interactions reliable under real-world latency and varied model response times. Handling Linux display/server differences (X11 vs Wayland), especially for pinned always-on-top overlays.

🏆 Accomplishments
A working end-to-end voice-first assistant that can execute meaningful computer tasks. A clean tool-routing architecture that makes the assistant extensible.

📚 What we learned
Accessibility is not a "feature"; it has to shape every architecture decision. Reliability beats novelty in voice UX: buffering, retries, and fallbacks matter more than flashy demos.

🚀 What's next
Improve conversational memory and personalization across longer time horizons. Expand desktop integrations (more apps, richer system controls, and tighter shortcut workflows).

title: AegisFS
summary:
🎯 What it does
AegisFS utilizes a kernel-level security middleware layer to enforce multiple layers of defense: 🛡️ Agentic RBAC Sandbox: Token-based authentication with granular permissions (Read, Write, Admin) and...

💡 Inspiration
This exact dilemma inspired AegisFS. Imagine an AI agent with write access to your filesystem accidentally deletes your SSH keys, overwrites your .

🔧 How it was built
Language: Rust (Edition 2021) with tokio for high-performance async I/O. Interface: ratatui + crossterm (Interactive TUI) and clap (Headless CLI).

⚡ Challenges
FUSE Cross-Platform Parity: FUSE is natively a Linux kernel feature.

🏆 Accomplishments
Zero-Trust Kernel Integration: By mounting via FUSE on Linux, any process is forced through AegisFS security logic, providing true system-wide protection.

📚 What we learned
Ownership for Safety: Rust's ownership model provides memory safety without sacrificing the performance needed for intensive file I/O.

🚀 What's next
WinFsp Integration: Bringing native, kernel-level FUSE support to Windows users without requiring WSL.

One major challenge was deciding how to group files logically. Folder structure alone is not enough, because related code can be spread across many parts of a repo.

In the future, we would like to improve the accuracy of domain clustering, add stronger file-to-file relationship detection, and make the flow animation more detailed.

Debates shape how people think, but there's no referee. Bad logic slides through, false claims get repeated, and confidence beats truth.

Solo build, 36 hours. Designed the system in Google Stitch, then scaffolded React 18 + TypeScript + Vite + Tailwind.

⚡ Challenges
Rate limiting was brutal. Each message triggers a philosopher response, fallacy analysis, and fact-check, all hitting Gemini.

API orchestration matters more than the API itself. Making four Gemini integrations coexist without stepping on each other taught me more about async architecture than any course.

Voice cloning so you can debate against a friend's actual voice. Multi-round structured debates with opening statements, rebuttals, and closing arguments.

title: Case
summary:
🎯 What it does
Case is a RAG-powered legal assistant for Canadian refugee law.

💡 Inspiration
Imagine fleeing your home country, only to be handed a dense, 50-page legal decision dictating your future.

🔧 How it was built
We built Case as a full-stack system: Frontend: Next.js + TypeScript + Tailwind for a clean, intuitive user experience.

⚡ Challenges
Grounding vs. fluency: Keeping responses helpful while strictly preventing uncited or hallucinated claims, which we managed through rigid system prompting.

🏆 Accomplishments
We didn't just build a generic chatbot, we built a trustworthy legal information tool with strict safety constraints for a high-impact domain.

📚 What we learned
Domain-specific RAG quality depends heavily on data prep and chunking strategy. Strong prompt constraints and output schemas are critical for trust in legal contexts.

🚀 What's next
Finish and harden the /ask flow end-to-end (frontend ↔ backend ↔ retrieval ↔ generation). Add stronger citation validation and fallback handling for low-retrieval-confidence queries.

title: Phalanx
summary:
🎯 What it does
Phalanx is an autonomous agent fleet that detects CVEs from the open web, forks your dependency state into N parallel remediation hypotheses via Ghost zero-copy forking, coordinates agents via Redis S...

💡 Inspiration
Supply chain attacks cost $60B annually. When a critical CVE drops, the industry standard is 60 days to remediate.

🔧 How it was built
Eight sponsor tools, each architecturally load-bearing — removing any one breaks the product: WunderGraph Cosmo: Federated supergraph with per-tool OAuth scopes via @requiresScopes.

⚡ Challenges
Ghost fork timeouts under load when 16+ stale forks queued. Solved by cleanup automation and bumping timeouts to 30s.

🏆 Accomplishments
The parallel-speculative pattern is genuinely novel. No existing tool forks state N ways, validates in parallel live backends, and cancels mid-flight on false positives.

📚 What we learned
Agent governance isn't optional — it's the product. Enterprise buyers don't care how fast your agent patches if they can't audit every decision.

🚀 What's next
Production multi-service deployment on Railway (Cosmo Router + Chainguard verification containers) with the dashboard on a Node host.

Jac's Object-Spatial Programming maps naturally to dependency graph analysis.

🔧 How it was built
Vettra is built as five distinct layers, each designed to solve a specific problem in the agent authorization pipeline. Layer 1: The Interceptor.

⚡ Challenges
Step-up authentication versus agent latency. Agent tool calls typically time out within 5 to 15 seconds.

🏆 Accomplishments
The Rust WASM verifier runs in production. It is not a proof of concept.

📚 What we learned
There is no standard format for agent action bundles. Any authorization system built for AI agents has to reinvent that metadata layer from scratch.

🚀 What's next
Multi-agent support with authorization policies for agent-to-agent delegation chains. Real-time risk adaptation that adjusts scoring based on agent behavior patterns within a session.

title: Drygate
summary:
🎯 What it does
You paste or upload your exported n8n workflow JSON. Drygate runs a four-stage pipeline and tells you if it is safe to ship.

🔧 How it was built
Next.js 14 for frontend and API routes.

⚡ Challenges
The n8n API is not what you think. The public REST API has no execution trigger endpoint.

🏆 Accomplishments
The fail-closed scoring rule. A hardcoded secret caps the score at 40.

📚 What we learned
n8n's internal API is fragile and undocumented. Building on it means reverse-engineering the editor.

🚀 What's next
The sandbox coverage gap is the biggest open problem. Credential-blocked nodes skip execution so most real workflows get 0 to 8 percent coverage.

Our vision is to transform ReflexionOS into a full operating system for autonomous intelligence.

Achieving ( AUC = 0.

Feature engineering matters more than model complexity. The 7-day and 14-day precipitation accumulations, anomaly scores, and seasonal encodings contributed more to AUC than hyperparameter tuning.

Full circuit solver.

title: Echo
summary:
🎯 What it does
You open Echo, tap the logo, and type what's on your mind. A few seconds later: "847 people have felt something like this.

💡 Inspiration
Has anyone here had a thought they were too ashamed to say out loud? Most of us have.

🔧 How it was built
Echo runs on a three-stage AI pipeline designed around a strict privacy constraint: raw thought text must never persist on any server, in any form.

⚡ Challenges
The hardest architectural decision was holding the privacy line at every step. It's easy to accidentally log a request body for debugging, or pass the wrong variable to an external API.

🏆 Accomplishments
The privacy architecture is something we're genuinely proud of. We designed for the worst case: assume the entire infrastructure is breached.

📚 What we learned
Building under a strict privacy constraint is a design superpower, not just a limitation. Every feature we couldn't build because of the privacy model forced us toward a better alternative.

🚀 What's next
We want to grow the dataset. The more people use Echo, the more useful it becomes — and the more resolution notes accumulate, the more powerful the "what helped" feed gets.

⚡ Challenges
Calibrating the risk assessment was our biggest challenge. Early versions of Civica had every single Specialist flagging every policy as high risk.

🏆 Accomplishments
The risk reports actually hold up to scrutiny. When we ran our default test policy — "Canada builds 500,000 new homes over 3 years" — the system didn't just say "housing is expensive.

📚 What we learned
AI agents are only as good as their architecture. We learned that throwing 50 identical agents at a problem doesn't give you 50 perspectives — it gives you the same answer 50 times.

Stronger models, deeper data.

title: ShieldClaw
summary:
🎯 What it does
ShieldClaw is the result of weaving Auth0's security and authentication measures through every step of OpenClaw's workflow, combined with Backboard.

🔧 How it was built
Auth0 JWKS (JSON Web Key Sets) ShieldClaw fetches Auth0's public JWKS endpoint to validate every incoming token against Auth0's actual signing keys before any business logic runs.

⚡ Challenges
Getting Auth0 FGA to sit correctly between the agent and Backboard so that permission checks happened before any data was ever retrieved took a lot of iteration.

🏆 Accomplishments
We built a system where even if an AI agent gets manipulated or hijacked, it is physically blocked from doing anything destructive because the permissions do not exist at the authorization layer.

📚 What we learned
We learned that the right way to secure an AI agent is not to monitor what it does after the fact, but to make dangerous actions structurally impossible before they can even be attempted.

🚀 What's next
Auth0 CIBA (Client-Initiated Backchannel Authentication) The next step is implementing Auth0's CIBA flow so that any high-stakes action like deletions or purchases triggers a push notification to the...

Backend: TypeScript + Node.js + Express with endpoints for request, confirm, and health.

⚡ Challenges
Portability: Initial hardcoded local paths for tooling caused environment-specific failures. Runtime stability: Emulator orchestration is heavy and sensitive to startup timing.

🏆 Accomplishments
Built a working end-to-end flow from user request to ranked ride options and booking confirmation. Successfully orchestrated multi-app checks in parallel instead of manual app-hopping.

📚 What we learned
Agent systems need strong guardrails, explicit contracts, and deterministic orchestration logic. Reliability engineering matters as much as model capability in real-world automation.

🚀 What's next
Harden orchestration reliability with retries, health checks, and better observability. Improve portability by fully externalizing runtime/tooling configuration.

🎯 What it does
Drawn lets you create your own anime hero and watch them battle iconic bosses. You draw your character mid-air using hand gestures tracked by your webcam.

💡 Inspiration
We wanted to build something that makes you feel like a kid again. That feeling of drawing your own superhero on the back of a notebook and imagining them in a real fight.

🔧 How it was built
The frontend is React with Vite and Tailwind CSS. For hand tracking, we use MediaPipe's HandLandmarker running on the GPU through the browser's webcam.

⚡ Challenges
Hand tracking was brutal at first. Raw landmark positions from MediaPipe are noisy, so early drawings looked like they were made during an earthquake.

🏆 Accomplishments
The hand tracking drawing actually feels good. When you pinch to draw and see smooth strokes appear in the air in real time, it genuinely feels magical.

📚 What we learned
MediaPipe in the browser is incredibly powerful but the raw data needs a lot of post-processing to be usable.

🚀 What's next
Multiplayer battles where two players draw heroes and face off against each other.
techStack: cloudinary, computer-vision, elevenlabs, express.js, google-gemini, javascript, mediapipe, mongodb, react, tailwind, veo

title: EchoAccess
summary:
🎯 What it does
EchoAccess converts inaccessible web forms into a guided voice conversation: Sign in with Supabase Auth Select a form (TD Bank, TTC Disability Card, CRA Benefits) Gemini parses the HTML and extracts e...

💡 Inspiration
Over 1.5 million Canadians live with significant vision loss, and the forms they depend on most (banking, transit, government benefits) are the hardest to use.

⚡ Challenges
Parsing real-world form HTML was messier than expected; each form had different structures, nested fieldsets, and inconsistent labeling, so we leaned heavily on Gemini's ability to interpret messy mar...

🏆 Accomplishments
We built a fully functional accessibility tool that handles three real Canadian government and banking forms end-to-end with voice.

📚 What we learned
True accessibility goes far beyond adding aria-label to buttons. It means rethinking the entire interaction model from the ground up.

🚀 What's next
We want to expand beyond three demo forms to support any arbitrary web form via URL input. Paste a link, and EchoAccess parses and voice-guides it automatically.
techStack: backboard-io, elevenlabsapi, fastapi, framer-motion, gemini, python, react, shadcn-ui, supabase, tailwindcss, typescript, vite
